subtopic: Seismic waves, magnitude and intensity, eruption controls, tsunami generation and spatial risk

content.issueStory: A geophysical event becomes a disaster only when its energy intersects exposed and vulnerable people. The same magnitude earthquake can have radically different consequences depending on depth, distance, geology, construction and preparedness.
content.coreConcept: Earthquakes release stored elastic strain along faults; volcanoes transfer magma and gases to the surface; tsunamis arise from sudden displacement of a large water column. Hazard intensity, propagation and damage follow different physical controls and must not be collapsed into one risk category.
dimensions: Seismic source: focus depth, fault mechanism and released energy shape shaking and wave paths.
Wave behaviour: P waves travel through solids and fluids, S waves through solids only, and surface waves often produce severe shaking.
Measurement: magnitude represents event size, while intensity records observed effects at a place.
Volcanic style: magma viscosity, silica, gas content and tectonic setting influence effusive or explosive eruption.
Tsunami process: vertical seabed displacement generates long waves that slow, shorten and rise near shallow coasts.
Risk geography: exposure, local geology, bathymetry, coastal shape, building quality and warning time control losses.
content.constitutionalLink: Article 21, the Disaster Management Act, 2005, seismic building codes, land-use regulation and the Indian Ocean tsunami warning system frame state responsibility for risk reduction.
content.dataReport: Primary uploaded sources: STATIC_02_Physical Geography.pdf volcano and earthquake sections plus PYQ_02_Geography.pdf questions on seismic measurement, wave properties and tectonic hazards. This note uses stable conceptual relationships and PYQ patterns; update event-specific data from current official sources before using it in a live mains answer.
content.caseStudy: The 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami showed how a megathrust earthquake, basin-wide propagation and low coastal warning capacity converted seabed displacement into transnational catastrophe.
argumentsFor: Monitoring networks improve detection and warning.
Hazard microzonation supports safer land use and construction.
Volcanic terrains can provide fertile soils and geothermal resources.
argumentsAgainst: Earthquake prediction at exact time and place remains unreliable.
Warnings cannot compensate for weak buildings and unsafe siting.
A large undersea earthquake does not automatically generate a destructive tsunami.
content.pyqLink: PYQs test P-S wave properties, shadow zones, magnitude versus intensity, volcanic forms and the conditions needed for a tsunami. The safest method is to identify the transmitting medium and displacement mechanism.

answerFramework.intro: Earthquakes, Volcanoes and Tsunami Geography is best understood as a spatial process in which origin, controlling conditions, resulting pattern and human significance must be connected.
body: Differentiate earthquake, volcanic and tsunami generation mechanisms.
Use a source-path-site framework to explain hazard propagation.
Separate hazard from exposure and vulnerability in the damage analysis.
Recommend monitoring, zoning, resilient construction, warning and community evacuation.
answerFramework.conclusion: A high-scoring geography answer converts process into pattern, locates the pattern with examples, and closes with risk-sensitive or resource-sensitive planning.

mainsExamples: Bhuj 2001 for severe intraplate earthquake risk.
Barren Island as India’s active volcanic example.
Japan for integrated earthquake-resistant design and tsunami preparedness.
prelimsFacts: The focus is the point of rupture within Earth; the epicentre lies vertically above it.
P waves are compressional and can pass through solids and liquids.
S waves are shear waves and do not pass through liquids.
Magnitude is a source measure; intensity varies from place to place.
Tsunamis have long wavelengths and low open-ocean heights but grow in shallow water.
Shield volcanoes are generally broader and less steep than composite volcanoes.

revisionBox.prompt: What is the central process chain in Earthquakes, Volcanoes and Tsunami Geography?
revisionBox.answer: Earthquakes release stored elastic strain along faults; volcanoes transfer magma and gases to the surface; tsunamis arise from sudden displacement of a large water column. Hazard intensity, propagation and damage follow different physical controls and must not be collapsed into one risk category.

revisionBox.prompt: What is the fastest prelims elimination rule?
revisionBox.answer: Do not say every undersea earthquake causes a tsunami.

revisionBox.prompt: Which example best anchors a mains answer?
revisionBox.answer: Bhuj 2001 for severe intraplate earthquake risk.

revisionBox.prompt: How should the conclusion move beyond description?
revisionBox.answer: Recommend monitoring, zoning, resilient construction, warning and community evacuation.

story: A geophysical event becomes a disaster only when its energy intersects exposed and vulnerable people. The same magnitude earthquake can have radically different consequences depending on depth, distance, geology, construction and preparedness.
core_concept: Earthquakes release stored elastic strain along faults; volcanoes transfer magma and gases to the surface; tsunamis arise from sudden displacement of a large water column. Hazard intensity, propagation and damage follow different physical controls and must not be collapsed into one risk category.

branches.label: Process
children: Seismic source: focus depth, fault mechanism and released energy shape shaking and wave paths.
Wave behaviour: P waves travel through solids and fluids, S waves through solids only, and surface waves often produce severe shaking.

branches.label: Pattern
children: Measurement: magnitude represents event size, while intensity records observed effects at a place.
Volcanic style: magma viscosity, silica, gas content and tectonic setting influence effusive or explosive eruption.

branches.label: Application
children: Bhuj 2001 for severe intraplate earthquake risk.
Barren Island as India’s active volcanic example.
Japan for integrated earthquake-resistant design and tsunami preparedness.

branches.label: Traps
children: Do not say every undersea earthquake causes a tsunami.
Do not confuse magnitude with locally observed intensity.
Do not infer eruption explosivity from cone height alone.

P waves are compressional and can pass through solids and liquids.
S waves are shear waves and do not pass through liquids.
Magnitude is a source measure; intensity varies from place to place.
Tsunamis have long wavelengths and low open-ocean heights but grow in shallow water.
Shield volcanoes are generally broader and less steep than composite volcanoes.

pyq_refs.question: PYQ pattern: explain the process and spatial consequences of Earthquakes, Volcanoes and Tsunami Geography.
pyq_refs.answer: Use a labelled diagram, causal sequence, regional examples and a planning implication.
pyq_refs.pattern_note: Mains rewards mechanism plus spatial expression, not a catalogue of terms.
